title: Unified Training of Universal Time Series Forecasting Transformers (Moirai)
authors: Gerald Woo, Chenghao Liu, Akshat Kumar, Caiming Xiong, Silvio Savarese, Doyen Sahoo (Salesforce AI Research; Singapore Management University)
tokenization_method: Non-overlapping PATCH tokenization with a MULTI-PATCH-SIZE input/output projection. A univariate series is split into contiguous patches; each patch (a length-p vector of raw values) is linearly projected to a d_model token. Crucially there is not one patch size but a learned bank of projection layers, one per patch size in {8,16,32,64,128}; the patch size used for a given series is chosen by a frequency->patch-size lookup table (large patches for high-frequency data to cut quadratic attention cost while keeping a long context; small patches for low-frequency data to push more computation into the Transformer). Only one set of projection weights per patch size, shared across frequencies that map to the same patch size. The forecast-horizon patches are replaced by a single learnable [mask] embedding (masked-encoder pretext). Output tokens are decoded by the matching multi-patch-size output projection into mixture-distribution parameters. (Non-learnable) reversible instance normalization is applied to inputs/outputs.
feature_types_handled: numerical (real-valued series) + multivariate (arbitrary variate count) + dynamic covariates. Each variate (target or time-varying covariate) is a channel that is patchified and flattened into the shared sequence; a 'dynamic covariate' (known-future values) is just another variate whose horizon patches are NOT masked. NO native categorical encoding, NO static-attribute fusion (static covariates are an explicit gap; the paper lists tabular/text multimodal inputs as future work). No explicit missing-value modeling beyond instance norm + masking.
token_granularity: temporal patch — one token = one fixed-length window (8/16/32/64/128 timesteps) of ONE variate. NOT per-value, NOT a learned codebook. Tokens are dense continuous projections, not discrete codes (contrast Chronos value-bins / TOTEM VQ codes).
numerical_strategy: raw values within a patch -> single linear (patch-size-specific) projection to d_model, after reversible instance normalization. Continuous-in / continuous-out; the model predicts continuous distribution parameters (no binning, no quantization). This is a regression head, not regression-via-classification.
categorical_strategy: none — no categorical input path exists. Variate IDENTITY (which channel a token belongs to) is encoded not as a categorical embedding but via the binary attention bias in Any-variate Attention (same-variate vs different-variate learned scalars), which is the closest analogue to a 'field-ID' signal.
high_cardinality_handling: n/a (no categorical features). Note: the any-variate design deliberately AVOIDS a per-variate learned embedding table (which would cap the variate count and break permutation invariance) — it uses two shared learnable scalars u(1),u(2) per head/layer instead, so the 'cardinality' of variates is unbounded with zero extra parameters. That is the transferable trick for high-cardinality field IDs.
temporal_handling: the whole model is temporal: patch tokens carry time order via RoPE (rotary position embedding indexed by time position i-j) inside attention; forecast horizon handled by masked-encoder [mask] tokens decoded in parallel (not autoregressive). Context/prediction lengths are not fixed — sampled from a task distribution at pretraining (total seq <= 512 patches, prediction length 15-50% of the window) so the model accepts arbitrary context/horizon at inference.
scales_to_many_features: Partially. Any-variate Attention is the scaling mechanism for the variate axis: flatten d_y variates x L patches into one sequence and let full self-attention model cross-variate interactions, with variate identity injected by binary attention bias. Pretraining samples variate count from a beta-binomial(n=128,a=2,b=5), mean ~37, MAX 128 variates; univariate sub-datasets are randomly concatenated into synthetic multivariates to teach cross-variate behaviour. COST: attention is over (variates x patches) flattened, so it is O((d_y * L)^2) — quadratic in the PRODUCT. The paper explicitly states 'limited support for high-dimensional time series' as a limitation; >128 variates or many patches blows up the sequence. So it scales to dozens of variates, not natively to hundreds-of-features-x-long-context.
downstream_model: Encoder-only (masked-encoder) Transformer with full self-attention, no autoregressive decoding. LLM-style internals: pre-normalization, RMSNorm replacing all LayerNorms, query-key normalization, SwiGLU FFN (hidden dim adjusted for equal params), no biases anywhere. Three sizes: Small 6L/d384/14M, Base 12L/d768/91M, Large 24L/d1024/311M (all d_kv=64).
pretraining_objective: Maximize log-likelihood of a MIXTURE of parametric distributions (the flexible-distribution contribution). Mixture components: (i) Student's-t (robust general), (ii) negative binomial (positive counts), (iii) log-normal (right-skewed), (iv) low-variance normal (high-confidence). NLL loss over the mixture; optimizing MLE of a flexible distribution is competitive with target-metric optimization and lets ANY downstream metric be evaluated post-hoc. Trained on LOTSA with sub-dataset capped sampling (cap eps=0.001 then renormalize) to fight domain/frequency imbalance, and sequence packing to raise effective batch size. AdamW lr 1e-3, wd 1e-1, batch 256; Small 100k steps, Base/Large 1M steps; A100-40G TF32.
key_results: (1) In-distribution Monash benchmark: a SINGLE Moirai (any size) beats every Monash baseline (each of which is trained per-dataset/per-series) on aggregate normalized MAE. (2) Zero-shot probabilistic forecasting on 6 held-out datasets (not in LOTSA): Moirai-Base/Large get best-or-second-best CRPS/MSIS on all but Walmart and Istanbul Traffic, beating full-shot PatchTST, TiDE, TFT, DeepAR that were trained+tuned on each target. (3) Zero-shot long-sequence (ETT/Electricity/Weather, avg over horizons 96/192/336/720): competitive with or beating full-shot iTransformer/TimesNet/PatchTST etc. (4) Performance keeps improving with longer context (100->5000), a property conventional forecasters lack. Model: Small14M/Base91M/Large311M; LOTSA = 27,646,462,733 observations, 9 domains.
baselines_compared: Statistical: SeasonalNaive, AutoARIMA, ETS/Theta/SES, TBATS. Deep full-shot: DeepAR, PatchTST, TiDE, TFT, iTransformer, TimesNet, Crossformer, DLinear, SCINet, FEDformer, N-BEATS, WaveNet, CatBoost, etc. Other pretrained/zero-shot forecasters (Table 1 comparison only, not head-to-head due to no open weights at the time): TimeGPT-1, ForecastPFN, Lag-Llama, TimesFM, TTM, LLMTime.
transferable_idea: Encode FIELD/VARIATE IDENTITY for an arbitrary, unbounded number of fields using a parameter-free binary attention bias (same-field vs different-field learnable scalars per head) plus rotary position for the ordered (time) axis, instead of a fixed-size learned embedding table — then flatten everything into one sequence so a single full-attention Transformer models cross-field interaction. This is directly portable to 70+ heterogeneous tabular features: treat each feature as a 'variate', tokenize it, and use a binary 'same-feature / different-feature' bias to keep the model permutation-invariant and uncapped in feature count, while reserving the value-projection for the numeric content. Secondary transferable idea: a multi-projection bank selected by a property of the feature (here frequency; for tabular it could be feature TYPE) so one model serves heterogeneous inputs.
limitations: (1) Multi-patch-size frequency mapping is admittedly heuristic (hand-set frequency->patch-size table). (2) Limited support for high-dimensional series — flattening makes attention quadratic in variates x patches, so it does not natively scale to hundreds of channels. (3) No static-covariate path and no categorical/text/tabular inputs (named as future multimodal direction). (4) Tenuous scaling: Large is not consistently better than Base on long-sequence (calls for proper LTM scaling laws). (5) Almost no hyperparameter tuning done (resource constraints); inference-time tuning of context length + patch size is needed per dataset, i.e. it is not fully turnkey zero-shot.
pitfalls: Mixture is over a FIXED set of 4 component families (Student-t, neg-binomial, log-normal, low-var normal) — 'flexible distribution' does not mean learned/nonparametric, just a 4-mixture; ablation shows replacing it with a single Student-t worsens norm MAE 0.655->0.740. The biggest ablation hit is removing multi-patch-size (0.655->1.156) and removing LOTSA diversity (->0.809), i.e. DATA DIVERSITY and the patch bank matter more than Any-variate Attention (0.655->0.904) — do not overclaim the attention bias is the dominant component. 'Zero-shot' results still require per-dataset inference-time selection of context length {1000..5000} and patch size on a validation set, so it is not metric-free zero-shot. Cross-variate modeling is learned partly from SYNTHETIC multivariates (randomly concatenated univariate series), which may not reflect real feature correlation structure. The any-variate flatten means real per-feature semantics are only distinguished by a binary same/different bias, not by feature meaning — two different categorical fields are indistinguishable beyond 'not the same column'.
relevance_to_topic: High-value mechanism source for the time-varying half AND for the scaling-interaction layer: its Any-variate Attention (binary field-ID bias + flatten + full attention) is the cleanest published recipe for feeding an UNBOUNDED, permutation-invariant set of heterogeneous channels into one transformer without a per-field embedding table — directly the 70+-feature problem — but it offers no categorical/static-feature tokenizer and its quadratic cost in (fields x length) is the assumption most likely to break in the topic's setting.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/SalesforceAIResearch/uni2ts (Apache-2.0, actively maintained as of 2025). Pretrained weights on HuggingFace (Moirai-1.0/1.1-R small/base/large, plus later Moirai-MoE-1.0-R and Moirai-2.0-R-small); LOTSA dataset published on HuggingFace.
